title: The Inevitable Decline of the US Oil and Gas Settler Era
content:
Despite recent, confident pronouncements that the global oil business is forever changed, history suggests these volatile market conditions will pass. Analysts heard very similar prognostications years ago when oil and gas prices spiked dramatically following a major international military invasion. However, that specific geopolitical crisis was eventually followed by years of decelerating demand, abundant energy supplies, and highly subdued prices. Nevertheless, there are strong, undeniable indications that the coming year could usher in a much more decisive, permanent industry shift. A sustained stretch of higher prices and the frantic scramble for new supply sources could hasten the end of the boom.

For many years, top industry analysts have constantly pondered where the next big energy finds will actually come from. One of the absolute least likely places for new massive growth is from mature, heavily depleted onshore fields in North America. Production from densely drilled shale basins, which already supply a massive portion of the world’s oil, simply cannot grow endlessly. In fact, official government energy agencies previously forecast that domestic oil output would permanently peak before slowly and inevitably fading. Consequently, the domestic oil industry has consolidated heavily, forced to grind out profits through incremental efficiency gains rather than discoveries.
